blocks.text: Jennifer Frazier, Chief Operating Officer, Sanchez-Baker
Re: Retained Search for a Director of the Board
Dear Ms. Frazier:

This letter confirms the terms on which Northgate Talent Partners LLC will conduct a retained search for a new independent director of the board of {{fact:f:E-2017-001.client}}. We are ready to begin on {{fact:f:E-2017-001.start}}, and we expect the search to run about four months from that point. Where this letter is brief, it is because the point is simple. Where it is exact, it is because it binds us both.
Scope of the Engagement

The search is for one director. Our first work is not sourcing; it is agreement on what you are hiring for. We will meet with you and with the directors who will vote on the appointment, and we will produce a written position specification covering the mandate of the seat, the experience the board is short of today, the committee work the director will be asked to carry, and the standards against which candidates will be measured. That document comes back to you for approval before any candidate is approached. Boards that skip this step spend the last month of a search relitigating the first week of it.

With the specification approved, we will map the market of qualified directors, develop a long list, and approach candidates on a confidential basis. You will receive a written slate of candidates the firm is prepared to stand behind, each assessed against the specification and against our own view of fit, rather than a stack of resumes with a covering note. We will manage the interview calendar, take up references on any candidate your board intends to appoint, and stay with the search through the decision and the acceptance. Our work is not finished when the slate is delivered. It is finished when a director is seated.

Two practical notes on how we work. We do not present a candidate we have not met ourselves, and we do not pad a slate to make it look like a market. If the qualified population for this seat proves to be small, you will see it at its true size and hear plainly what the market for this profile is, rather than be handed the appearance of choice. Second, the board's own availability will govern the calendar more than anything the firm does. Slates go stale. The candidates we bring you will be in play elsewhere, and the search will need decisions at the pace we agree at kickoff.
The Search Team

James Grant, Senior Consultant, will lead the search and will be your day-to-day contact; he holds the pen on anything that reaches a candidate. Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant, will draft the position specification and build the market map. Jason Bell, Research Associate, will run sourcing and first contact. I will review the slate before it reaches you and I will attend the board presentation myself. If the search is not going the way this letter describes it, you will hear that from me, early, and in plain words.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the engagement is {{fact:f:E-2017-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope described above. We bill it in three equal installments: the first at the start of work, the second on delivery of the slate, and the third on the appointment or on the close of the search, whichever comes first. The fee is not contingent on an appointment. You are retaining the firm for the work, and the work is done whether or not your board elects to seat one of the candidates we bring you. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt. Reasonable out-of-pocket expenses, principally travel and any candidate travel we arrange at your request, are billed at cost and separately from the fee; no expense of consequence will be incurred without your approval in advance.
Off-Limits and Replacement

For twelve months following the close of the search we will not recruit the director we place, and we will not approach an executive of {{fact:f:E-2017-001.client}} on behalf of another client. If the director we place resigns or is removed for cause within twelve months of election, we will conduct one replacement search for the same seat at expenses only. A firm unwilling to write that clause is telling you something.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from the start date set out above until the search is closed, either by an appointment or by your instruction. Either of us may end it on written notice. If you terminate, you owe the installments already invoiced together with a pro rata share of the current one for work performed to the date of notice, and nothing further. If we conclude that the search cannot be completed against the specification as approved and the board is not prepared to revise it, we will tell you so and stop, rather than continue to bill against work that will not produce a director.
Confidentiality

What we learn about your business, your board, and your succession thinking stays with the search team and is disclosed to no one else. The obligation runs the other way as well, and that is the more delicate half. The people we approach are sitting executives and sitting directors, and most of them will have told no one at their own company that they took our call. In anything that circulates beyond the decision-makers on your side, candidates are described by their current role rather than named. We ask that your board observe the same practice, and that materials we send you are not forwarded outside the group that will vote. A leaked slate does not merely embarrass this firm. It costs the candidate something neither of us can give back.
Limitation of Liability

We will perform the search with the care and skill customary in our profession, and we take the assessments in our slate seriously. We do not warrant the future performance of any director your board appoints; that appointment is the board's decision, made on its own judgment and its own interviews as much as on our work. Our aggregate liability arising out of this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it, and neither of us will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential damages. Nothing in this paragraph limits liability that cannot be limited by law.
Governing Law

This letter is the entire agreement between us with respect to the search and supersedes anything discussed before it. It may be amended only in writing signed by both of us, and it is governed by the laws of the state in which our firm maintains its principal office, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules.

If this letter reflects our understanding, please sign below and return one copy to my attention. We will begin on the date set out above, and I will introduce the team to you before the first board meeting.
Sincerely,
Memorandum
To: James Grant, Jeffrey Patterson
From: Jason Bell
Re: Kickoff, Board Director Search for Sanchez-Baker

The firm is retained by {{fact:f:E-2017-001.client}} to place one independent director on the board. Work begins {{fact:f:E-2017-001.start}} and the search is scheduled to run about four months. The seat belongs to the board, not to management, which changes who we have to satisfy and how the calendar works. James Grant leads and owns the client relationship. Jeffrey Patterson owns the position specification and the market map. I own sourcing and first contact. Jennifer Frazier, Chief Operating Officer at Sanchez-Baker, is our day-to-day contact and controls board scheduling, which is going to matter more than anything else on this list.

The objective is one director elected to the board inside the term of the engagement, chosen from a slate we can defend name by name. Everything below serves that and nothing else. Two measures I care about: how many of the people we want to talk to will take the call, and how long the board takes to answer once they have. The first is our problem. The second is the one that sinks searches, and it is the one we have the least control over, so we start managing it now rather than in the last month.
Workstreams

items: Specification. Jeffrey Patterson drafts it after the board interviews, not before. Nothing goes out until the board signs it off. Draft to James Grant first.
Market map. Sitting and recently retired directors, plus operators with board-ready profiles in adjacent sectors. Jeffrey Patterson builds it; I feed him names as they surface from calls.
Sourcing and first contact. Mine. I work the phone. Assume most of the useful names come from people who are not themselves candidates, which is the part that does not show up on a list.
Client management and calendar. James Grant. Board availability across the summer is the risk on this search, and Jennifer Frazier is the only person who can fix it.
Off-limits. Confirm with Jennifer Frazier which companies are out of bounds before I start calling into them. Easier to ask now than to unwind an approach later.
Discretion. No candidate named in anything that circulates. Current role only, in every document that leaves the team, including our own notes.
Slate assembly and presentation. James Grant and Jeffrey Patterson, working from my contact notes on every candidate we approach.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Board interviews to be scheduled through Jennifer Frazier. Ask for the chair and the directors who will vote, not the whole board. A room of nine produces a specification nobody wrote.
Jeffrey Patterson to circulate a specification outline before those interviews, so we ask the right questions in the room rather than reconstruct them from memory afterward.
I start calls this week. Not waiting for the map to be finished. The map gets better from the calls, not the other way round.
James Grant to get a read on what this board will actually approve, and on whether it has approved anything like it before. That is not in the letter and we should not learn it from a rejection.
Every approach logged the day it happens. If it is not in the log, I did not make the call, and by August none of us will remember.

blocks.text: One thing worth saying at the start. Sanchez-Baker has told us the seat is open. The board has not yet told itself what the seat is for, and until it does, any name we put forward is a guess wearing a resume. That is normal at kickoff and it is the entire reason the specification comes first. Expect someone to ask for names in week two. Push back. Questions to me or to James Grant, and if it is about the calendar, it is James Grant.
Jennifer Frazier, copy to Jeffrey Patterson and James Grant.

Quick update ahead of our call, with one ask at the bottom. The specification is approved and has not moved since, which makes the approaches cleaner than they usually are at this stage. Jeffrey Patterson has the market map at the point where it is useful rather than merely long. We are working through the priority names now and I have made first contact with a good share of them.

Two things worth knowing early. Interest is real, but the profile question is not settled. The people who match the specification most exactly are already sitting on two boards and are the hardest to move; the people with the most capacity are a step earlier in their careers than the specification implies. James Grant and I would rather test that with you now than surface it in a slate. Nothing here is off track for {{fact:f:E-2017-001.client}} at this point. It is the ordinary shape of the market for a seat like this one.

The ask: we need the directors who will vote available for candidate conversations, not only for the interviews at the end. The candidates worth having are asking who they would be sitting with, and that question gets answered by a person or it gets answered by silence. If Sanchez-Baker can give us dates now, I will build the calendar around them.
Jason Bell
Minutes of Working Session
Attendees

items: James Grant, Senior Consultant, Northgate Talent Partners (chair, minutes)
Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant, Northgate Talent Partners
Jason Bell, Research Associate, Northgate Talent Partners
Jennifer Frazier, Chief Operating Officer, Sanchez-Baker

blocks.text: Discussion

Purpose of the session was to review the long list against the approved specification and to decide who we approach. Jeffrey Patterson opened with the map. The useful result was not the size of the list but its shape: it sorts into three groups and only two of them are worth our time. Sitting directors at larger companies match the specification exactly and will be the hardest to move, largely because their calendars are already spoken for. Operators one step short of a first board seat have the capacity and the appetite, and will require the board to carry a small amount of risk. The third group, the professional director set who will take any seat offered, we agreed to leave where we found it. Jennifer Frazier did not need persuading on that point.

The conversation then turned to what {{fact:f:E-2017-001.client}} is actually solving for with this appointment. Jennifer Frazier was direct about it: the board wants operating experience from a company that has crossed the size Sanchez-Baker is crossing now, and it wants someone who will ask management hard questions without turning every meeting into a review. Jason Bell reported from first contact that the second group responds to precisely that framing, while the first group asks about committee load before anything else. That split is useful. It should shape how we write the approach, not only whom we call.

One caution from the chair. We spent part of the session on individual names, and at this stage I would rather we did less of that. Approaches are underway and candidates are in conversation. A name spoken of warmly in a working session has a way of becoming an expectation by the time the slate lands, without anyone having agreed to it. Nothing in this record identifies a candidate: current role only, as the engagement letter provides, and I would ask that the same discipline hold in whatever goes back to the board.

On timing, we are entering the stretch where the calendar rather than the market decides the outcome. Jennifer Frazier will confirm which directors sit on the interview panel and will hold time for them. Jeffrey Patterson raised references early, as he tends to, and he is right to. For a board seat the references worth having are people who have sat in a boardroom with the candidate, and those cannot be arranged discreetly at short notice. We start that groundwork now rather than at the end.
Action Items

rows: Confirm the directors sitting on the interview panel and hold time for them
Continue approaches across the two priority groups; log every response the day it happens
Recut the map against the operating-experience test the board set today
Confirm the off-limits list against the board's own affiliations
Begin discreet groundwork on boardroom references for the likely candidates
Reset the board's expectation on slate size and the true depth of this market
Circulate the weekly approach summary by current role only, no names

blocks.text: Next working session to be scheduled through Jennifer Frazier. Minutes recorded by James Grant; corrections to me within the week or the record stands.
Prepared by Jason Bell, Research Associate. Reviewed by James Grant, Senior Consultant.
Status Summary

The search for a director of the board of {{fact:f:E-2017-001.client}} is at its midpoint and is on schedule. The position specification is approved and has not moved since approval. The market map is complete and has been recut once against the operating-experience test the board set at the working session last month. Approaches are underway across both priority groups, first conversations are done with the majority of the priority names, and panel interviews are being scheduled now. We expect to present a slate on the timeline agreed at kickoff. Nothing in this report asks the board for a decision it has not already been asked for.

The one thing worth flagging at the halfway mark is that the search has consumed its easy half. The work behind us was ours to control: writing the specification, building the map, making the calls. The work ahead runs on the board's calendar and on candidates who have their own reasons and their own timing. Searches of this kind are not usually lost in the sourcing. They are lost in the four weeks between a slate landing and a board answering it. We would rather say that now, while it is an observation, than later, when it would be an excuse.
Accomplishments This Period

items: Specification approved and used unchanged in every approach since. Jeffrey Patterson's draft needed one round, which for a board specification is one round.
Market map complete, then recut once against the operating-experience test. The third-group names came out by agreement rather than being left in to pad the count.
First contact made across both priority groups. The operator group responds at the better rate of the two, consistent with what we reported last month.
Interview panel confirmed and time held. This was the item most likely to cost the search a month, and it did not.
Confidentiality holding. No candidate has been named in any document circulating outside the search team; candidates appear by current role only, including in our own working papers.

Reference groundwork started early on the likely candidates. For a board seat the references worth having are people who have sat in a boardroom with the candidate, and they take time to arrange without drawing attention.

blocks.text: Upcoming Work

items: First-round panel interviews. James Grant attends each one.
Slate assembly. Jeffrey Patterson and James Grant write the assessments; every candidate on the slate will have been met by the firm before it reaches the board.
Slate presentation to the board, on the timeline set at kickoff. James Grant will ask for a decision date in the room rather than leave the board to find one later.
References on any candidate the board intends to appoint, once the panel has narrowed the field and not before. Approaching a candidate's boardroom references early is how a search becomes public.

Support through offer and acceptance, including the conversation with a candidate's own chair where one is needed. Jeffrey Patterson will hold the withdrawal note in reserve; if a finalist steps back, the client hears it from us the same day and hears what happens next in the same message.

blocks.text: Budget

The engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2017-001.fee}}, billed in three equal installments, of which the first has been invoiced. The second is triggered by delivery of the slate rather than by a date, so it will fall where the work falls. Effort to date is consistent with the fee and there is no change in scope to report or to anticipate. Out-of-pocket expenses remain within the ordinary range for a search of this kind, and none of consequence has been incurred without approval. If the board revises the specification in a way that reopens the market, that is a scope conversation, and James Grant will raise it before any work is done against it rather than after.
Risks

items: Board calendar. The panel dates are held, but summer holds are the first thing to move. If a date slips, candidates who are in play with other boards do not wait for us. James Grant re-confirms the panel ahead of each interview.

Profile tension. The candidates who match the specification exactly are the hardest to move; the ones with real capacity are a step earlier. The slate will reflect both. If the board is unwilling to consider the second group, then the market for this seat is thinner than the specification implies, and that conversation should happen before the slate rather than during it.

Discretion. Every additional person inside Sanchez-Baker who knows the search is running is another route by which a candidate's name reaches their own board. That has not happened. Keeping it that way means materials do not travel beyond the directors who will vote.

Late withdrawal. On a board search it is ordinary for a strong candidate to step back near the end, for reasons that have nothing to do with the client. We plan for it by keeping the slate live rather than collapsing to a single name early.
